macro_segments.title: The Debate between Rav and Shmuel: Hevel as a Requirement for Liability in a Bor

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Rav-Shmuel debate on whether hevel (air pressure) is necessary for liability
Analysis of the Mishnah: Why is there exemption for wells less than ten tefachim when an animal dies
Gemara's reasoning: There is not enough hevel to cause death, but enough to cause damage
Student question and discussion: How can ten tefachim cause death when the animal's head is above that height

macro_segments.title: The Case of the Ox Falling into an Irrigation Canal: Rav Nachman's Ruling on Treifa

micro_segments.title: Story of an ox that fell into a six-tefach-deep irrigation canal and was immediately slaughtered
Rav Nachman declares the animal treifa due to potential internal injuries from impact
Rav Nachman's teaching: The owner would have saved his ox if he had studied Torah and learned the halacha
Analysis: The canal was only six tefachim deep, challenging the Mishnah's ten-tefach requirement

macro_segments.title: Rava's Challenge to Rav Nachman: The Execution Site and Height Requirements

micro_segments.title: Rava challenges Rav Nachman with the baraita about the execution site being two stories high plus the person's height
Gemara's response: Even if impact under ten tefachim can cause death, execution requires greater height for certainty
Quotation of Rabbah bar Abbuha on the commandment to love your neighbor: execution method must be merciful
Gemara's resolution: Making it too high would disfigure the body, so there is a precise balance needed

macro_segments.title: The Roof Overhang Case: Defining a Bayit by Interior vs. Exterior Measurements

micro_segments.title: Baraita on the obligation to build a fence: when public domain is ten tefachim higher than your roof
Question: Why is ten tefachim the measure for a bayit when the interior must be ten tefachim, not the exterior fall
Gemara's resolution: The house is defined by interior measurement; exterior can be less if ground is dug deeper

macro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's Calculation: The Ox's Height Plus Canal Depth Equals Ten Tefachim of Impact

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman's reasoning: An ox's underside is four tefachim from the ground; the canal is six tefachim deep
The total impact experienced by the falling ox is ten tefachim, making Rav Nachman's ruling consistent
Resolution: The Mishnah speaks of an ox rolling into the pit, not one falling from standing position

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two Partners' Well: Determining Liability When Both Dig

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: A pit owned by two partners; first doesn't cover, second doesn't cover; who is liable
Gemara's opening: How can two people be joint owners of a pit liable for damages
Possible scenarios for two partners' joint liability: both appoint an agent, or both dig equally
Principle: One cannot appoint an agent for transgression; personal responsibility remains with the digger

macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rabbi and Rabbanan: Who Is Liable When Two People Complete a Pit to Ten Tefachim

micro_segments.title: Baraita: One digs nine tefachim, another completes it to ten; last person is liable for death
Rabbi's position: Last person liable for death; both liable for damages
Rabbanan's position: Last person is fully liable for both death and damages
Source analysis: The word 'ish' (man) in the verses teaches that one person can be fully liable even when two dig
Rabbi's counter-interpretation: The word 'ish' is not redundant; both phrases are independently necessary

macro_segments.title: The Plastering Case: Does Modifying an Existing Pit Transfer Ownership

micro_segments.title: Baraita: One digs ten, another comes and plasters and carves; last person becomes liable
Resolution: Plastering increases the hevel (air quality) even if pit was already ten tefachim
Alternative explanation for Rabbi: First digger did not create sufficient hevel for death, second did
Alternative explanation: First digger had no effective hevel due to wide pit; second created lethal cavity

macro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Does Reversing Your Act Return Responsibility to the First Person

micro_segments.title: Rava's question: If someone filled in the pit they completed, returning it to nine tefachim, whose liability is it now
Two possible approaches: either you reversed your act and returned responsibility, or you now own a nine-tefach pit
Conclusion: Teiku—the question remains unresolved

macro_segments.title: Water in the Pit: How Water's Lethality Affects the Pit's Status

micro_segments.title: Teaching: An eight-tefach pit with two tefachim of water is as lethal as a ten-tefach dry pit
Principle: Each tefach of water is as injurious as two tefachim of air
Unresolved question: Does this principle apply to nine tefachim pit with one tefach of water
Unresolved question: Does it apply to seven tefachim pit with three tefachim of water

macro_segments.title: Expanding a Well: Does Widening a Pit Decrease or Increase Liability

micro_segments.title: Question: If one person dug a well and another expanded it, does expansion increase or decrease hevel
Rav Ashi's resolution: Look at how the person actually died and where they fell
If death by hevel, expander is exempt; if death by impact, expander is liable
Alternative: Liability depends on which side of the pit the person fell into

macro_segments.title: The Well's Proportions: Hevel Depends on Whether Pit Is Deeper or Wider Than Deep

micro_segments.title: Debate: One opinion—hevel exists as long as pit is not wider than it is deep
Opposing opinion: Hevel exists only when pit is deeper than it is wide

macro_segments.title: The Mishnah on Shared Well: When Does the First Partner Become Exempt From Liability

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: First person passed and didn't cover; second person passed and didn't cover
Gemara asks: What must occur for the first person to be exempted from liability
First opinion: First person is exempt when second person starts using the well
Second opinion: First person must explicitly hand over the cover or responsibility to second person

macro_segments.title: The Case of Two Water-Drawers: Implicit Transfer of Responsibility

micro_segments.title: Baraita: First person drawing water; second says 'leave it to me, I'll draw'; first becomes exempt
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov: First person must explicitly hand over the cover/bucket
Gemara's analysis: The dispute concerns whether breira (retroactive determination) applies to responsibility

topical_tags.term: treifa (animal unfit due to internal injury)
nestalku maaseh rishon (previous act is nullified)
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov (requires explicit handover)
